coredata.dc:title: Latest advances and status analysis of nanomaterials for microalgae photosystem, lipids and biodiesel: A state of art
coredata.dc:creator: Reetu,
Clifford, Mike
Prakash, Rajiv
Rai, Monika Prakash
coredata.dc:description: Growing energy demand and exhaustion of mainstream fuels lead to the need for renewable energy sources. Microalgae possess remarkable properties including carbon dioxide mitigation, accruing intracellular lipids and an ability to thrive in harsh conditions, which makes them a potential feedstock for biofuel production. However, narrow photosynthetic light absorption and limited lipid production are major bottlenecks that remain key obstacles to practical commercialization. Therefore, previous research has suggested that the use of nanomaterials could overcome these restrictions. Their extraordinary physicochemical characteristics, as well as an enormous surface area, can be exploited in practical applications. Some nanomaterials are reported to promote the photosynthetic efficiency of microalgal cells, which in turn increases biomass production. Furthermore, by using specific nanoparticles, an advancement in lipid production can also be achieved. A specific group of nanomaterials target the upregulation of the key enzymes involved in the lipid synthesis pathway, which in turn leads to higher lipid production and accumulation. However, most of the studies deciphered their toxic behaviour. This review endeavours to deliver an overarching insight into the impact of nanomaterials on microalgae photosynthesis, lipid and biodiesel production. It also focuses on the use of algal residue in possible applications that makes the process largely sustainable with zero waste. This review surveys the state of the combination of nanotechnology and microalgae to achieve high biomass and lipid production along with other valuable products.

row.entry: Malaysia has postponed its 20% biodiesel blending rule for at least six months in some areas. In June 2021, the government plans to expand 20% biodiesel blending to Sabah, and in December 2021, to Peninsular Malaysia.
Because of Covid-19-related difficulties, Indonesia's government has postponed the implementation of its 40% biodiesel mandate from 2021 to at least 2022.

Due to a surge in biodiesel prices, which have nearly doubled since July 2020 owing to increased expenses for the soybeans necessary to manufacture biodiesel, Brazil temporarily reduced its biodiesel obligation from 13% to 10% in April 2021.
The 20% ethanol blend has been put on hold for an indefinite period, due to financial issues.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Growing energy demand and exhaustion of mainstream fuels lead to the need for renewable energy sources. Microalgae possess remarkable properties including carbon dioxide mitigation, accruing intracellular lipids and an ability to thrive in harsh conditions, which makes them a potential feedstock for biofuel production. However, narrow photosynthetic light absorption and limited lipid production are major bottlenecks that remain key obstacles to practical commercialization. Therefore, previous research has suggested that the use of nanomaterials could overcome these restrictions. Their extraordinary physicochemical characteristics, as well as an enormous surface area, can be exploited in practical applications. Some nanomaterials are reported to promote the photosynthetic efficiency of microalgal cells, which in turn increases biomass production. Furthermore, by using specific nanoparticles, an advancement in lipid production can also be achieved. A specific group of nanomaterials target the upregulation of the key enzymes involved in the lipid synthesis pathway, which in turn leads to higher lipid production and accumulation. However, most of the studies deciphered their toxic behaviour. This review endeavours to deliver an overarching insight into the impact of nanomaterials on microalgae photosynthesis, lipid and biodiesel production. It also focuses on the use of algal residue in possible applications that makes the process largely sustainable with zero waste. This review surveys the state of the combination of nanotechnology and microalgae to achieve high biomass and lipid production along with other valuable products.

ce:section.ce:para: The crescent population is sternly influencing the balanced ecological environment, consequently inducing demands for all the necessary resources. Pre-eminently, magnified industrialisation is creating the foremost issue of global warming and climate change [1]. Moreover, the unfettered use of fossil fuels has reared sustainability issues and jeopardised its shelter of supply. As per the statistics, worldwide the energy demand has grown up by 5.8% in 2021, repealing the significant decline during the covid-19 lockdown [2]. Total primary energy consumption is steadily rising, with a projected increase of 57% between 2010 and 2040 [3]. Currently, the total energy demand effectuated by fossil fuels is around 70–80% [4]. Hence, the CO2 emission by the combustion of exhaustive resources cannot be overlooked as it has reached a mark of 24 Gigatonnes [5].

The existence of the global energy crisis is at the forefront, therefore, United Nations also declared “Sustainable Energy for all” in 2001 to combat the challenge. Also, to ensure the availability of high-quality, sufficient, reliable, and sustainable energy to all by 2030, tackling global energy poverty was kept as SDG 7 [6]. The demand for primary energy in the Non-OECD and Asian countries would increase in the coming two decades because of the increasing population, as depicted in Fig. 1. except for the United States, China, and OECD countries as a consequence of ongoing innovations [7].

The development of renewable energy should be considered as the critical alternative to fossil fuels to meet the global energy demand. Liquefied natural gas, petroleum gas and electric substitutes require exorbitant investments in new machinery. Besides these renewable energy resources, biodiesel is the most suitable reserve for petroleum-derived fuel as it does not require any modification to the existing machinery as well as it is eco-friendly and technically feasible [8]. The aforementioned advantages have stimulated the generation and use of biodiesel in the transportation industry by many countries.

Biodiesel production and use have begun in Asian countries such as China, India, and Malaysia, just as it has in Europe and the United States. For example, the government of India had set a goal of using 5% biodiesel (B5) blended with diesel fuel by 2030, whereas B5 blending has been initiated in China and B10 blending standards are under discussion [9]. Malaysia's government announced that B10 (10% biodiesel blended with diesel fuel) will be introduced in 2019, with B20 to follow in 2020 [10]. As summarized in Table 1, by the report of the IEA, policy implementation has been slower in many countries due to the Covid-19 pandemic and related inflation [11].

However, many biofuel policies are being framed and implemented among countries around the globe to promote biofuel application. In a recent report by IEA Bioenergy Task 39, different policies were assessed to determine their scale of effectuality to encourage the production and usage of biofuels [12]. The prime takeaway message from the study was that these policies have enhanced the biofuel market growth worldwide. Technology-push policies are aiding the preliminary phase progression like research and capitalisation whereas Market-pull policies like blending mandates, exemptions and excise reductions are supporting to create the demand for biofuels. For accelerating the enactment and dissemination of advanced biofuel technologies, a hybrid of demand-pull and technology-push strategies is essential. The nations with leading growth in the production and consumption of transport biofuels have integrated both the market-pull and technology-push approaches [12,13]. Despite the significant advancement in technology, suitable policies are further required to escalate commercialisation.

Since every country is aiming for sustainable development and a secure energy future, therefore the majority are considering, biodiesel as a possible replacement for diesel fuel since it is non-toxic, biodegradable, and renewable [14]. However, more than 75% of the biodiesel production cost is toward the cost of feedstock [15]. In the present scenario, plant seed oil is a major source of biodiesel production [16–18]. Biodiesel from microalgal biomass is referred to as the third generation, which is considered the best alternative mainly because it does not alter the food chain and leads to lesser pressure on the arable lands as well as the environment [19,20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microalgae cultivation and potential of nanoadditives
ce:section.ce:para: Biodiesel can be derived from various sources preferably the non-edibles (jatropha, jojoba, castor) as they eliminate the ‘food vs fuel’ issue. Non-edible crops are known to be of forest origin, making harvesting, gathering, and transportation difficult. In addition, the seasonal availability of non-edible crops and ineffective marketing channels are key hindrances for establishing biodiesel manufacturing enterprises. The presence of high FFAs and moisture content necessitates a pre-treatment before the transesterification to obtain the final product [8].

Microalgae have been identified as a third-generation biodiesel feedstock and a viable replacement for fossil fuel-derived diesel. It is an important group of microscopic unicellular organisms able to perform photosynthesis and convert solar energy into chemical energy. They are a diverse group of microorganisms comprising both the photosynthetic prokaryote namely cyanobacteria and eukaryotes such as green microalgae mainly Chlorophyta [21]. These microorganisms can thrive in harsh conditions and are common in many locations. The variety of habitats lead to biochemical diversity amongst microalgal species. These diverse bioproducts from microalgae are used as supplements in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, food, nutraceutical, biofuels, and feed applications. Since microalgae capture the greenhouse gas CO2 and can convert it into biomass, they are considered as one of the strongest candidates for efficient biofuel production. Their cultivation requirements are very simple, comprising light, sugars, CO2, potassium, nitrogen, and phosphorus which facilitates growth and the production of lipids in a significantly shorter time in comparison to other feedstocks [22]. Hence, CO2 augmentation is one of the key factors regulating the harvestable biomass yield.

Notwithstanding many efforts towards the commercialization of microalgae biofuel production, low biomass productivity is still the biggest challenge. Along with the low biomass productivity, poor harvesting efficiency further limits the economical energy production from the microalgal cultivation system [20]. This is probably due to less light-harvesting, narrow absorption within the solar spectrum and light shading during growth in large-scale cultivation. Moreover, a suitable economically viable lipid enhancement strategy remains elusive. To overcome these bottlenecks, nanomaterials containing photocatalytic characteristics have been introduced into microalgae cultivation systems to augment the photosynthetic rate and lipid productivity.

The maximum solar energy conversion efficiency evaluated theoretically was found to contrast with the actual measurements of productivity [23]. The absorption of light by chloroplast is restricted to 380–700 nm of the spectrum and ingression to around 50% of the incident solar light [24]. Moreover, the photosynthetic apparatus can be saturated by less than 10% of full sunlight [25]. Photosynthetic organisms evolved for reproductive success and to exclude competition, which leads to a lack of photosynthetic eﬃciency. Several factors can be associated with this low efficiency including an inadequate light source, nutrient status, ROS generation, wasted irradiance or inhibition of algal growth from excess [26]. Thus, the lipid productivity of microalgae is inadequate to meet the requirement of commercial applications [27].

Current research is centred on improving synthesis and augmentation of lipids and other valuable compounds from microalgae. Disparate approaches have been established from material science and bioengineering to increase the efficiency of lipid production, photosynthesis and minimise irradiance wastage [28]. Various research has claimed that nanomaterials, photobioreactor design, and genetic engineering approaches, such as generating mutant algal cells by changing their antenna size, stress due to culture conditions, metals can elevate lipid synthesis and photosynthetic efficiency in microalgal cells [29,30]. Although these stress conditions can also have negative effects on microalgae growth, therefore the research oriented on modulation of stress conditions and suitable optimization is required.

However, the significantly higher surface area and remarkable stability of nanomaterials have the potential to modulate microalgae growth, metabolism, and photosynthetic efficiency [31]. Various literature illustrates the implementation of nanomaterials like carbon nanotubes to ameliorate microalgae viability and enhance lipid content, but their toxic nature is a major constraint and restricts growth [32]. In one of the recent studies, carbon nanomaterials were added to plant cells, and it was demonstrated that their photosynthetic activity was enhanced by around 30% [33]. This could be due to the biocompatibility of carbon nanomaterials that increased visible light absorption by the chloroplast.

Thus, nanomaterials can be added at different steps as shown in Fig. 2 where they can act as synthetic photocatalysts to microalgal cells which makes them a strong candidate for lipid accumulation and biomass production. Nanomaterials were termed “Magic Bullets” by Paul Ehrlich [34]. Their size range of 1–100 nm leads to unique properties [35]. Nanomaterials can penetrate specific cellular locations, or facilitate adsorption through their additional surface area. The nanomaterials can exist naturally in mineral composites, volcanic dust, human-generated waste materials such as coal combustion, and fumes generated by welding [36]. Nanotechnology can help in enabling novel as well as an increased functional attribute in phototrophs. This would also lead to the enhanced harnessing of solar radiation.

This comprehensive review attempts to assess the potential of disparate nanomaterials in several microalgal schemes, to enhance photosynthetic performance as well as lipid production of microalgae by extending the availability of the spectral region for oxygenic photosynthesis. Here, we further unravel the abilities and risks associated with nanotechnology and how it can enhance microalgal photosynthetic efficiency for sustainable energy production, increased biomass, biodiesel, and value-added compounds associated with algal residue. Economic aspects, major challenges and future directions of the technology are also discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of nanomaterials on microalgal cell system
ce:section.ce:para: Materials that possess at least one dimension less than 100 nm are described as nanomaterials. They can be natural in existence or can be engineered. Exotic applications and peculiar properties have attracted much attention to ENMs [37]. ENMs are referred to as nanoparticles which are intentionally structured, with customization in sizes and composition, to meet specific goals. There are various strategies to synthesize nanoparticles with desirable properties. Mechanical synthesis can be achieved using mechanical milling, laser ablation, inert gas condensation etc; whereas methods like chemical vapour deposition and hydrothermal synthesis are suitable chemical synthesis routes [38]. Addressing microalgae research, green synthesis of nanomaterials is possible, with sources such as plants, microalgae, microbes, or any other biological entity. Green nanoparticles are said to have reduced toxic effects on biological targets as well as to have a lower risk of contaminating the environment (ecotoxicity) [37]. The advancement in the use of ENMs is a result of the exclusive electrical, optical, and magnetic properties they possess. They possess remarkably large surface area to volume ratios, which contributes to additional “surface” dependent properties. Nanotubes, dendrimers, quantum dots and fullerenes are some prevalent nanomaterials.

Due to their widespread properties, many studies have been done on the repercussions of nanomaterials on different microalgal species in various applications. Nanomaterials primarily influence microalgae cell endurance, enzymatic-based photosynthetic activity, lipid content and morphological characteristics [39]. Wei et al. (2010) studied the impact of f-MWCNT on marine microalgae Dunaliella tertiolecta and reported a reduction of 5% and 22%, in PSII yield post-exposure to 5 and 10 mg/L of f-MWCNT respectively [40]. The study conducted by Blaise et al. (2008) confirmed the results of the previous study, they examined nanoparticle toxicity on green alga Pseudokirchneriella subcapitata [41]. When exposed to stress, the microalgal cell produces ROS such as singlet oxygen (1 O2), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide radical ( . O2) as well as hydroxyl radical ( . OH). Disproportionate accumulation of ROS leads to damage of membrane lipids, proteins as well as genetic materials and triggers suppression in cell growth [31].

Studies have depicted slow algal growth in the number and in terms of biomass after they are exposed to carbon nanomaterials [42]. The agglomeration or physical interaction of nanoparticles with the surface of the cell leads to a reduction in growth. The intracellular accrued carbon nanomaterial causes insufﬁcient nutrition supply as well as reduced availability of light. This restriction in supply results in the suppression of algal growth and viability [43].

The size of the nanoparticle also influences its toxicity; as was reported in a study by Saxena et al. (2020) on Coelastrella terrestris [44] . Nanomaterials also play a significant role in protecting microalgae against several types of abiotic stresses. The nanomaterials mimic the activities of antioxidative enzymes and quench free radicals [45]. However, when present in the growth medium, nanomaterials induce oxidative stress and cause the deterioration of chlorophyll, photosynthesis, and biomass. The unique physicochemical properties of nanomaterials improve the photosynthetic growth of microalgae. They also induce a mild condition of stress for the accumulation of lipids without adversely affecting the cells. Various studies have concluded that nanoparticles like iron and magnesium act as a nutrient in the culture medium [46]. The study also observed that nanoparticles of Iron produce several ROS via a Fenton-type reaction which induces oxidative stress to the microalgae [47].

Furthermore, an increase in the growth of Chlorella vulgaris and Chlorella sp . KR-1 have been shown to occur as a result of exposure to Mg-amino clay nanoparticles [48]. When mixotrophic cultivation of Chlorella vulgaris was supplemented with MgSO4-NPs, an enhancement in photosynthetic activity and reduction in glycerol consumption was observed. Also, MgSO4-NPs reduced light penetration leading to the flocculation of microalgae in turn inducing an increase in chlorophyll content [49].

Likewise, nanomaterials embedded inside chloroplasts possess the capacity to alleviate ATP synthesis via photosystems by making use of their distinctive electronic as well as optical properties. Additionally, under the presence of adequate solar radiations, the thylakoid protein-pigment complexes absorb a greater number of photons than, that can be directed into electron flow [72]. The electronic bandgap of the nanomaterials permits the transformation of this assimilated solar light into excitons by transferring electrons to the photosystems, leading to an upsurge in the photosynthetic efficiency [73]. Along with the enhancement in the PSE, it leads to the generation of ROS which has a deteriorating effect on the photosynthetic unit [74]. On the contrary, it is also said that this ROS generation elevates lipid production and accumulation [51].

In a study on Chlorella vulgaris, an increase of 17% and 21% in growth and biomass respectively was observed in the vicinity of pristine carbon dots. However, the reported observations were for a concentration below 10 μg/mL, whereas at higher concentrations the pristine carbon dots inhibited the growth of microalgae [55]. Khanra et al. (2018) investigated the effects of CNS, and NGS on Chlorella pyrenoidosa and reported that NGS had no inhibitory effect on the microalgae growth, it alongside was most suited for increased lipid production. The lipid concentration increases in the presence of undoped carbonized nanomaterials, CNS and NGS [58].

Ren et al. (2020) observed a significant increase in biomass of 3.18 g/L and lipid content of 40.26% when Scenedesmus sp. was cultivated in the presence of SiC nanoparticles with a photoperiod of 6:18 h (light:dark) [51]. Adding SiC nanoparticles to the microalgal culture accelerated the substrate expense and induced stress atmosphere, thus augmenting the acetyl-CoA carboxylase activity and lipid biosynthesis. They also suggested that illumination with a xenon lamp along with SiC nanoparticle addition promoted microalgal growth and lipid accumulation. Similarly, Fu et al. (2020) studied the biological reaction of Chlorella to nano metal oxide [50]. The study revealed that the microalga had a wide tolerance and positive biological reaction to immobilized nanometal oxides, especially nano-ZrO2 fixed on D301, which showed a noticeable increase in growth. Immobilization appeared to have a detrimental effect on the toxicity of the nanoparticle.

Kadar et al. (2012) studied the impact of nanoparticles in the Pavlova lutheri and Tetraselmis suecica culture systems [71]. The nanoscale nZVI induced enhancement in lipid content along with normal growth. ZnO nanoparticles associated with microalgae showed an increase in growth patterns on sewage wastewater with significant enhancement in lipid productivity for biodiesel application [75]. Table 2 summarises research on the impact of various nanomaterials on microalgae.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of nanomaterials and microalgae photosystem
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthesis is a physiochemical process which occurs in the photosynthesis unit of a cell that is a portion of the thylakoidal membrane. The essential machinery like the photochemical reaction centre, electron carriers, and the enzymes required for generating NADPH and ATP is embedded in the thylakoid membrane [76]. The basic mechanism of oxygenic photosynthesis may be described by the steps in Fig. 1. in the plant, cyanobacteria, and microalgae the photosynthetic reactions are catalysed by four multi-subunit protein complexes comprising P680, P700, a cytochrome b6f (Cytb6f) oxidoreductase along with an ATP synthase [77].

Furthermore, PSII consists of a group of manganese ions conjugated with the D1 protein and remaining subunits coordinated with 40 kDa D2 protein. Both the D1 and D2 proteins contain 5 transmembrane helices [78]. The reaction centre is flanked by 14 chlorophyll-a bound CP43 and 16 chlorophyll-a bound CP47 proteins, responsible for light absorption containing 6 transmembrane helices each [79]. Photolysis of water is a unique process catalysed by PSII, which in turn provides the earth with almost all its oxygen. Oxygen is produced by a mechanism that involves two water molecules being oxidized. This oxidation of water molecules leads to the creation of molecular oxygen (O2) by absorbing four photons. P680, which includes two units of weakly attached chlorophylls, which function as the principal contributor of electrons.

Post assimilating the foremost photon, translocation of single e- occurs from P680 to an accessory chlorophyll pheophytin, later to QA quinone, and finally reducing quinone QB ( Fig. 3). Additionally, it oxidizes a tyrosine in the vicinity, then, it performs extraction of single proton and electron out a collection of four manganese ions further binding a couple of water molecules and single units of calcium, bicarbonate, and chloride ions as co-factors. After one more photochemical cycle, QBH2 is formed when a pair of protons are absorbed from the stroma by the doubly reduced QB (QB2–) [77]. The doubly reduced QB (QB2–) is further liberated by an oxidized quinone into the thylakoid membrane. As soon as another set of light reactions is over, a total of four oxidizing equivalents is provided to the Manganese cluster. These help in producing O2 by oxidizing two water molecules. The manganese cluster is then again set to its reduced state, which is denoted as S0 in the KOK–JOLIOT FIVE S-STATES reaction (S0–S4) [80].

Further, the electrons are translocated to Cytb6f then to PSI and ultimately accepted by NADP+ present in the stroma of the chloroplast. While this electron transportation takes place, the H+ ions are pumped inside the thylakoid lumen making it acidic. These protons pass through the ATP synthase (present in the thylakoid lumen) forming the end products of the light reaction of photosynthesis; that is NADPH and ATP [81]. Later, these energy-carrying entities are used to fix the CO2 into glucose. The products of photosynthesis are used for microalgal cell growth and survival. Hence, to accelerate growth, photosynthetic efficiency must be enhanced [82].

The image demonstrates the different components that are involved in the light reaction of photosynthesis it includes reaction centres PSI and PSII additionally various intrinsic and extrinsic protein complexes are also depicted; where Tyr bound D1, D2 and 43, 47 are reaction centre core proteins and core antenna proteins respectively. The red arrows from the oxygen-evolving complex (OEC) responsible for water splitting to Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) represents the linear flow of electrons; electrons are transferred via Pheo molecule to plastoquinone QA and QB, and eventually to PC through Cyt-b6f [83].

Despite having the exemplary property of converting sunlight into different biochemicals of significant use it holds various drawbacks restricting the microalgal competencies and decelerating the pace of evolution and commercialisation of valuable microalgal products. The dependence of bandwidth on the organic light absorption mechanism is among the limitations [84]. Various genetic engineering approaches have been attempted, with limited success due to the difficulty in modifying natural photosynthetic systems [84]. Nanotechnology emerged as a possible technique to address the drawbacks of microalgal photosynthesis [85]. The unique light capturing property of nanomaterials has been exploited and integrated into microalgal systems [86]. Nanomaterials can efficiently capture light in a broad spectrum that could be used to meet this objective. Nanomaterials can act as photocatalysts to enhance the light harvesting efficiency of antenna pigments at PS-I and PS-II, which results in improved photosynthesis like composites of CDs as well as pure CDs were proved to accelerate the photochemical reaction by efficient photocatalysis of H2O lysis [87].

As discussed, microalgal photosystems consist of antenna chlorophylls and RCs which are located inside the thylakoidal membrane held together by weak intermolecular forces. Sequential electron transport takes place starting from the antenna chlorophyll to RCs and so on to the further acceptors. The addition of nanomaterials to the microalgal cultures leads to an exciton enhancement effect. The conjugation of the nanomaterials with the photosynthesis machinery results in the absorption of a broad range of wavelengths [88]. Free oscillating electrons cause plasmon enhancement; a significant increase in the excited electrons is observed. This increase in the concentration of the excited electrons can be attributed to the electron-hole separation and the resonance of the free electrons [89].

It is also observed that the presence of nanomaterials triggers the elevated concentration of photosystem II reaction centre protein A and photosystem I chlorophyll apoprotein A1, which results in greater photosynthetic efficiency. Osterloh et al. (2017) also reported that the photocatalyst effect on photosynthesis is thermodynamically stable with ΔG < 0. GQDs are among one such list of materials, possessing a lower energy gap in comparison to the parent graphene molecule [90,91]. Here, the bandgap of GQDs is directly proportional to the dimensions altered by the quantum confinement effect. Notable results were observed for diameters under 10 nm [92]. In a study by Yadav et al. (2012) a graphene-based photocatalyst was synthesized and used to instigate artificial photosynthesis, a notable increase in PE was observed [93].

Moreover, studies have reported that certain atoxic nanomaterials can enhance photosynthetic efficiency in microalgae [94]. For instance, in a study done by Ren et al. (2020) accelerated hoarding of biomass and lipid content in Scenedesmus sp. was observed in response to SiC as well as g-C3N4 nanoparticles [51] . In another study by Giraldo et al. (2014), carbon nanotube-nanoceria complexes produced a three-fold enhancement in light absorption activity and boosted electron transport efficiency in plant chloroplast [33]. Nakada et al. (2019) concluded that the co-loading of Fe, Ru Ox cocatalyst, exhibits collective significant properties of RuOx as an active surface catalysis site and as a ‘collector’ of photons generated whereas FeOx as a ‘blocker’ for detrimental Fe2+ oxidation causing specific water oxidation in Z-scheme water splitting [95]. It was also noted that Cerium oxide NPs (nanoceria) can quench the ROS [96].

Estrada et al. (2022) investigated the impact of carbon coated zero valent iron nanoparticles containing 7.26% (wt%) of iron on the Chlorella sorokiniana, an increase of 47% in photosynthetic activity was observed in the presence of 70 mg/L of the nanoparticle. Further, the lipid and carbohydrate content also raised by 25% and 56% respectively [97]. Zhao et al. (2021) observed a considerable increase (by red light shifting) in the photosystem II activity of the Chlorella sp. in the presence of 1 mg/L of the carbon dots [98]. Light harvesting gold nanoparticles of 5 nm diameter cultivated with Chlorella zofingiensis resulted in the acceleration of the electron transport rate of the photosystem II, ultimately increasing the photosynthetic efficiency [99]. Graphene oxide quantum dots- Chlorella pyrenoidosa system was subjected to a blue light (465 nm) emission after ultraviolet (380 nm) excitation, the quantum dots absorbed the ultraviolet light and facilitated the photosynthetic activity [100].

SWCNTs acted as a photoprotective agent to the photosynthesis machinery of Chlamydomonas reinhardti, a concentration of 2 μg/mL of SWCNTs was proved to increase the photosynthetic electron transport rate by exchanging the energy between the redox active nanotubes and the photosystem [101]. Hence, from the foregoing review of the performed studies, the potential of nanotechnology as a key for promoting novel functional attributes in photosystems can be apprehended. This nano-bio interface permits efficient solar energy harnessing and biochemical sensing in the microalgae-based fuel system.

Some microalgae are well acknowledged for producing a considerable amount of oil. Energy obtained from microalgae is usually termed as third-generation biofuel whereas fuel from edible; nonedible plant parts are called as first- and second-generation biofuels, respectively. Production of fuels from microalgae is economically feasible compared to current biodiesel from plant counterparts [102]. Disparate microalgal species exhibit a wide variety in lipid productivities and contents of different types of microalgae species. As demonstrated in Table 3, high lipid content (> 50%) can be observed in species like Botryococcus braunii, and Parachlorella kessleri.

They are highly efficient in converting solar energy into chemical energy and their limited water requirements make them an excellent candidate for biofuel production. Although microalgae confer so many advantages, microalgal biofuel is expensive compared to alternative energy sources.

On the basis of protein homology search and comparing genomes, it is usually believed that the basic pathways of fatty acid and lipid synthesis are conserved in the green lineage [106]. In a photosynthetic cell, TAG requires several enzymatic reactions and occurs in different subcellular compartments. It involves independently regulated and physically separated steps, de novo fatty acid synthesis takes place in the Chloroplast and Endoplasmic reticulum (as outlined in Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: De novo synthesis of TAG in microalgae
ce:section.ce:para: The foremost step in the de novo synthesis of TAG in microalgae begins in the chloroplast. Within the chloroplast ACCase, the rate-restricting enzyme in fatty acid biosynthesis catalyses the conversion of acetyl-CoA into malonyl-CoA [107]. Then, malonyl-CoA trans acylase catalyses the transformation of malonyl-CoA into malonyl-ACP. Several FAT, which hydrolyses acyl-ACP into FFAs catalyse the cessation of the fatty acid chain lengthening. Production of DAG is led by the subsequent acylation of glycerol-3-phosphate in the chloroplast, by fatty acids (in the form of acyl-ACP). The DAG primarily acts as a precursor for photosynthetic membrane lipids, like galactoglycerolipids. These photosynthetic membrane lipids help the growth of more than 50% of the total glycerolipid [108]. On the other hand, the FFAs can be exported into the cytosol, where a LACS unites the FFA with a coenzyme A, resulting in the production of acyl-CoA. Finally, acyl-CoA can experience lengthening and desaturation steps in the endoplasmic reticulum [109]. Several studies mention that TAG synthesis, by the action of several acyltransferase isoforms, takes place in the endoplasmic reticulum and the chloroplasts. Glycerolipid biosynthesis is a conserved pathway between higher and lower plants which is depicted by the existence of genes homologous to higher plants acyltransferases in microalgae [108]. The TAG biosynthetic pathway as currently understood in green alga is shown in Fig. 4.

Acyl-lipid assembly occurs in the endoplasmic reticulum and in the plastids. It is largely accepted that a group of enzymes named acyltransferases catalyse the formation of triacylglycerols, by the sequential acylation of G3P backbone with three acyl-CoAs. In the 1950 s, Professor Eugene Kennedy and colleagues first described this pathway as the 'Kennedy Pathway'. An Arabidopsis Book chapter lately reviewed and covered the 'Kennedy Pathway' and its associated route from acyl-CoA to oil synthesis, under 'acyl-lipid metabolism' [108].

Biochemical activities and enzymes of this pathway have often been associated with the membranes of the endoplasmic reticulum where it is accepted as the cellular location for glycerolipid assembly. It starts off with the acylation of the sn-1 position of G3P by a GPAT, which results in the production of LPA [110]. Then, further acylation of LPA is achieved by the action of LPAT, resulting in the formation of PA. The elimination of the phosphate group from phosphatidic acid is catalysed by PAP, for generating DAG, the central intermediate of all glycerolipids. DGAT catalyses the final step to oil synthesis [111]. All three acyltransferases mentioned above use acyl-CoA as acyl donor. Hence, these reactions are considered as acyl-CoA-dependent. It has been disclosed that the gathering of metabolites such as glycerol, fructose, sucrose, and trehalose is triggered by stress in cultivation such as low temperature (less than 20 °C), nutrient (nitrogen) depletion, high metal concentration (Fe), etc. Such accumulation of metabolites maintains osmolality, whereas molecules which have a charge, including proline and glycine betaine, prevent water loss by readjusting the osmotic equilibrium [112]. However, useful insights on key regulators of lipid metabolism in microalgae can be achieved by understanding the mechanism of increased lipid accumulation as a response to stress. This leads towards a directed pathway of engineering to reduce the cost of bio-diesel production [113].

The dissimilarity in ACCase activity was studied in 7 days cultivation, in research by Ren et al. (2020) [51]. The ACCase activity grew gradually in the initial 4 days before it became stable. Whilst the entire culture period, ACCase activity in the SiC nanoparticles supplemented group was higher than that of the control and without SiC nanoparticles group. ACCase activity (174 pmol/L) reaches a maximum value in the presence of SiC nanoparticles on 4th day, while lesser ACCase activity was acquired in the control (136.6 pmol/L) as well as lacking SiC nanoparticles group (156.7 pmol/L). Thus, it was reported that the addition of SiC nanoparticles and light illumination would significantly increase the ACCase activity of microalgae. The synthesis of fatty acids forms the basis of routes of lipid synthesis in algal cells [114]. The initial step in fatty acid biosynthesis, namely, the transformation of acetyl-CoA into malonyl-CoA is catalysed by ACCase, which has been well known as an enzyme that restricts reaction rate [114]. The role of ACCase in enhancing lipid accumulation was previously shown in plants, and improved expression of ACCase has been proposed to be an eﬀective strategy to promote neutral lipid content [115]. Also, a study reported that oxidative stress produced in the synthetic treatment of nanoparticles and irradiation may increase lipid production [65].

In a study by Xi et al. (2022), Chlorella vulgaris was cultivated in the presence of 50 mg/L of Fe2CO3 nanoparticles and an increase of 15.12% was observed in the lipid content however, above the concentration, production was inhibited due to the generation of ROS [116]. In another investigation Chlorella vulgaris was supplemented with 50 mg/L of ZnO nanoparticles leading to the enhancement of 24.47 ± 0.03% in the lipid content [117]. Rana et al., (2020) attributed shift in the cellular metabolism as the key to lipid increase, when 30 mg/L of iron oxide nanoparticles were cultivated with Chlorella pyrenoidosa [118]. Aravantinou et al. (2020) explored the effect of ZnO nanoparticles along with the hydraulic retention time (HRT) on Scenedesmus rubescens. Although at low HRT the algal growth was inhibited but after a 40 days HRT there was no fall in the growth and the lipid content was increased to 28.5% in the presence of 0.081 mg/L of ZnO nanoparticles [119].

Vimali et al. (2022) achieved a total lipid content of 57.6% in the Desmodesmus sp. by using 75 mg/L of the magnesium oxide nanoparticles along with the nutrient stress. The rise in the lipid content was attributed to escalated activity of the ACCase enzyme triggered by the magnesium ions [120]. Hence following the application of nanoparticles, an increase in the accumulation of lipids was observed, which might improve the production of biodiesel. The production cost of biodiesel is directly affected by lipid content, hence a study of the factors affecting lipid production and accumulation could prove to be advantageous. There is a great potential to increase lipid content through the application of nanomaterials in microalgae cultivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanomaterial aided harvesting, lipid extraction and transesterification for biodiesel production
ce:section.ce:para: The continual increase in the population and need for renewable fuel sources has led to an immense increase in the study of biodiesel and the exploration of eligible sources. The cost of producing biodiesel is largely based upon certain critical factors, which are: the type of raw material, harvesting techniques and catalysts used. The type of raw material used for producing biodiesel accounts for 60–80% of the entire fuel price [121]. Assessing feedstock in terms of productivity, quality and exploitation of by-products is important for producing cost-effective biodiesel. Among microalgae, two main species, namely Chlorella vulgaris and Chlorella protothecoides, which contain high proportions of oil and have been studied by many researchers for biodiesel production [122]. Microalgal biodiesel primarily comprises fatty acids, which are mainly unsaturated in nature. Wastewater could be a potential feedstock, as a variety of microalgae species with different fatty acid profiles can be obtained from this algal biomass. By implementing this hybrid system approach, Bjerk produced biodiesel from microalgae Chlorella sp., Euglena sp., Spirogyra sp., Scenedesmus sp., Desmodesmus sp., Pseudokirchneriella sp., Phormidium sp, Nitzschia sp. [123].

Nanotechnology applications in obtaining microalgae biodiesel mainly include nanomaterial utilization in biomass cultivation, lipid accumulation, extraction, purification, and the conversion process as a catalyst in FAME production. Nanomaterial presence in the cultivation media is proven to be very beneficial for microalgae growth and lipid production ( Table 4). These can be considered as a competitor by the cells leading to stress generation and as a result of it, lipid accumulation. Along with growth stimulation, they can also be detrimental to the microalgal cells leading to toxicity and growth retardation. The detailed impact of nanomaterials on microalgae growth and lipid has been mentioned already.

The role of nanomaterials in the harvesting process is of great use (Table 4). Chlorella exposed to Aminoclay-conjugated TiO2 nanocomposites at a concentration of 3 g/L led to a harvesting efficiency of 85%, cells were also disrupted with an efficiency of 95% [124]. MNPs are often used in microalgae harvesting (Fig. 2). An interim agglomeration between cells and MNPs is the prerequisite for microalgal separation [125]. As a result of the electrostatic interactions between the negative surface charge of microalgal cells and MNPs, the external magnetic force attracts binding with the microalgae cells, leading to segregation. The process can be performed in a circuit with the detachment of magnetic flocculant and microalgae biomass [125]. Due to specificity in the surface area, magnetism, and biocompatibility characteristics, Fe3O4 NPs are most frequently used. Fe3O4 NPs surface charge is susceptible to pH; therefore, it can gain or lose protons to attain negative and positive charges, respectively inducing electrostatic flocculation ( Fig. 5) [126].

The process of nanoparticle recycling cannot be neglected as it tends to make the entire process of microalgae harvesting more economical. A study done by Wang et al. (2014) claimed that the recovery efficiencies of Fe3O4 NPs reached 95% in just 4 mins [127]. Vandamme et al. (2015) also achieved 100% separation efficiency by using cationic pyridinium groups doped onto cellulose CNCs [128]. CSNPs was used to separate Chlorella vulgaris with 90% flocculation efficiency [129]. The aforementioned strategies are simple and consume little energy. Easily available and inexpensive flocculants, can be applied in microalgae harvesting. Electrostatic attraction along with interactions like bridging, nanoscale adsorption and ion-exchange mechanism, accelerates the flocculation among microalgae and NPs offering low-cost harvesting processes.

Once the microalgal cells are efficiently harvested, the subsequent stage includes lipid extraction from the cells and conversion of this lipid to FAME via transesterification. The mechanical strength and chemical resilience of the microalgae cell is enhanced by the complex glycoprotein and carbohydrates that constitutes the cell wall. As a result, the operations for lipid extraction and cell disruption account for a major portion of the overall biodiesel production cost [130]. Many physical and chemical methods have been investigated for efficient lipid extraction. The yield of extracted lipid is substantially affected by the type of solvent used, after osmotic shock, homogenization, ultrasonication, and irradiation. These methodologies are energy expensive, contributing to downstream processing costs [91]. Recently, the nanomaterials as substitutes for the traditional extraction method has demonstrated not only a non-toxic, but also more effective lipid extraction (Table 4) [131].

Huang et al. (2016) investigated the effect of nickel oxide nanoparticles on the lipid extraction efficiency of the microalgae cells for 96 h. When compared to the control group, an increase of 208.37% was observed without the use of any high energy demanding equipment and pre-treatment [132]. In another investigation, ZrO2 nanoparticles were chose for the lipid extraction from Chlorococcum sp., an efficacy of 78.52% was achieved using 15 mg/L of the nanoparticles [133]. Bai et al. (2013) performed lipid extraction from Chlorella vulgaris/ Leptolyngbya sp. co-culture using silver nanofibers, they obtained an enhancement up to 136% [134]. Green synthesised silver nanoparticles were used for extracting the lipids from Chlorella vulgaris and it was improved from 8.44% to 17.68% by AgNPs mediated efficient rupturing of microalgae cell wall [135]. However, further in-depth evaluation is required for a thorough comprehension of this area, more significantly for industrial-level scaling-up.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanoadditives mediated transesterification for biodiesel production
ce:section.ce:para: Raw material lipids, mainly triacylglycerols/free fatty acids are converted into non-toxic and environment friendly biodiesel by a process known as transesterification [136]. The crude algal oil which has high viscosity is transformed into lower molecular weight compounds in the form of fatty acid alkyl esters [137]. During the transesterification process, when crude oil reacts with an alcohol (usually methanol) in presence of a catalyst, FAME form along with glycerol. Though acid catalysts are regarded as more advantageous, alkali catalysts provide a better commercial route, because of their speed [138] . Although various conventional methods have been employed for the transesterification of lipids, a lack of a suitable technique still exists.

The current approach is to use multifarious nanomaterials and to exploit their unique properties to improve the process economically. In a study, 99% of the FAME yield was obtained from the transesterification of algal lipid by using Ca (OCH3)2 catalyst. The reaction was performed at a temperature of 80° C for a duration of 3 h [139]. One of the latest studies by Nematian et al. (2020) reported a conversion rate of 69.8% [140]. This involved superparamagnetic nanoparticles Fe3O4 conjugated with Rhizopus oryzae lipase which constituted a bionanocatalyst, for the conversion of the lipid from the Chlorella vulgaris. Many different studies have been reported in Table 4 showcasing the nanomaterial employment at various stage of biodiesel production from microalgae.

Although nanomaterials have a significant impact in increasing the catalysis rate one cannot ignore the toxic effects. Extensive research is required to harness the full benefits of the technology. Furthermore, nanomaterials not only affect photosynthesis, lipid production, extraction, and catalysis rate, but many recent studies have suggested a substantial effect on the physicochemical properties of the resulting biodiesel.

A variety of nanomaterials can be exploited to enhance the quality and quantity of biodiesel. However, the quality of the fuel and the characteristics must meet standards and regulations in order to derive commercial benefit from microalgae-based biodiesel. In the recent past, Kalaimurugan et al. (2020) showed a profound effect of CuO2 nanoparticles on the biodiesel obtained from the Neochloris oleoabundans [161] . CuO2 was used at different concentrations and it was at 100 ppm that the performance and combustion properties such as kinematic viscosity, flash point and density etc. were substantially enhanced. The augmentation in the aforesaid characteristics is beneficial, for instance a higher flash point is attributed to improved safety and storage, reducing fire risks. Although density increase would lead to a fall in calorific value, further increasing the fuel consumption and hence making it economically less feasible [162]. Similar studies were performed on biodiesel procured from the alga Caulerpa racemosa. The fuel was supplemented with bismuth oxide (Bi2O3) nanoparticles; an improvement in the volatility and fall in the viscosity was reported. Lower viscosity leads to an increased heat release rate [163]. Praveen et al. (2020) analysed the exhaust emission of the Chlorella vulgaris derived biodiesel blended with Al2O3. A beneficial reduction was observed in NOx and CO emissions [164]. Table 5 compares standard diesel, microalgae derived diesel and microalgae derived diesel blended with the nanoparticles.

The cetane index is a measure of the ignition properties of the fuel; the higher the cetane index, the better the combustion characteristics. Kinematic viscosity is a dynamic entity dependent on the temperature and fatty acid profile; lower viscosity is preferred for an ideal fuel. Another property is the acid number which can be defined as the milligrams of potassium hydroxide required to neutralize one gram of fuel [164]. Additionally, total sulphur content, flash point and oxidative stability etc are some other properties which are taken into consideration for determining the utility of biodiesel. Hence, techniques like novel solvent combination, genetic engineering and nanotechnology etc. are being employed to increase the quality of microalgae biofuels and to make efficient commercialization possible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Defatted microalgal residue applications: biorefinery approach
ce:section.ce:para: There are many techno-economic constraints associated with algal biofuels. To improve commercial prospects, not only the lipid portion should be used but also the algal residue. Since algal cells comprise a major portion of proteins and carbohydrates, therefore use of the residue will make the overall technique more economically feasible [165]. Research into this area has been conducted by various researchers in recent times. Jeong et al. (2021) converted the 5 wt% defatted algal biomass of Scenedesmus obliquus into levulinic acid and formic acid with a yield of 39.93% and 25.82% respectively in 40 min [166]. H2SO4 was used with a concentration of 0.8 M as the catalyst at 180 °C and obtained valuable bio-based chemicals. Similarly, the biphasic solvent system defatted remnant of Dunaliella salina was transformed into 5-HMF and LA [167].

Likewise, various biorefinery approaches were also adopted to use defatted microalgae. The lipid extracted biomass of the microalga Nanochloropsis sp. was used as a protein-rich fish meal substitute for European sea bass [168]. Further Yun et al. (2020) used the acid hydrolysates of deoiled microalga biomass as a substrate for fermentation, to obtain biosurfactant from Bacillus subtilis C9 [169]. Khomlaem et al. (2020) co-produced polyhydroxyalkanoates and carotenoids using defatted Chlorella sp. biomass as a carbon source [170]. Another important property of microalgae is that they can act as “Bio-nano factories” that is nanoparticles could be synthesized from the microalgae [171]. Since nanoparticles are synthesized from biological substrates, they are eco-friendly and cost effective [172]. Microalgae are strong candidates for green synthesis.

Variety in size, morphology, and stability of nanoparticles can be sustained by optimizing parameters like pH, temperature, incubation time, and the substrate or solution concentration [173]. Past researches have claimed that these algal-based nanoparticles have a wide range of application in wastewater treatment, and possess antifungal, anticancer, and antibacterial activities, which can be exploited for therapeutic treatments [174]. Nannochloropsis oceanica lipid-free biomass was used to synthesize carbon nanofibrils with high stiffness and mechanical strength [175]. In a study by Yugay et al. (2020) silver nanoparticles were synthesised by using alginate, fucoidan, laminaran, obtained from microalgae Saccharina cichorioides and Fucus evanescens [176]. The cytotoxicity of the microalgae-derived material was tested using rat C6 glioma cells which were highest for the alginate and laminaran followed by fucoidan. Hence, defatted microalgae biomass can be exploited and holds great potential. However, more research is needed in this area.

According to the U.S. Energy Information Administration, the total annual consumption of petroleum is 35.4 billion barrels, whereas the world crude oil reserves account for 1650 billion barrels so oil reserves will be exhausted by 2064 [177]. Hence, there is an imperative need for an economically feasible renewable energy source. Microalgal biodiesel is an eligible candidate if certain shortcomings can be resolved. As the production of biodiesel from microalga does not require arable land, the food vs fuel issue is less of an issue than with other biofuels.

However, the financial viability of the entire process is a pressing concern to make the entire process commercially sustainable, there needs to be a major emphasis on reducing the costs associated with culturing and harvesting. As shown in Table 6, the nanomaterial-assisted biodiesel production system is less economical feasible in comparison to the conventional fuel as it is in the range of 59.64–259.26 INR/L whereas the conventional fuel ranges from 83.26–98.89 INR/L [178]. However, by developing processes with high-value by-products, balancing cost with energy can make algae-derived biodiesel achievable. Raeisossadati et al. (2019) opined that a reduction in the cost of fuel generated from microalgae by an increase in biomass and lipid productivity would make the final cost less than USD 0.7 per litre [179]. Vasistha et al. (2021) reported an elevation in the C16 and C18 FAMEs by using ZnO NPs in the microalgae culture [180]. This review has focused on the use of nanomaterials to elevate biomass and lipid productivity.

*Variation in the cost can be explained as it is determined by various factors like nanomaterial used, concentration of nanomaterial, carbon credits, production scale and value of the by-products generated.

Reasonable cultivation systems with high light penetration and low-cost nutritional cultivation methodologies are another prerequisite for affordable microalgae derived biodiesel [190]. It has been reported that by using Fe3O4-TiO2 nanoparticles coated with chitosan, harvesting and disruption of Chlorella minutissima can be achieved with a total cost of $7.53 per kg biomass, which also includes nanoparticles worth $7.175. This cost was estimated to be 3.7 fold lower than the cost of using conventional methods like disruption and drying, estimated as $27.69 per kg biomass [151]. Past studies convey the effect of various nanomaterials on the lipid profile and biomass of the microalgae. Furthermore, an excess of defatted biomass would be generated that could be repurposed, making the overall process cost effective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical implications, challenges and future directions
ce:section.ce:para: Microalgae are living entities, which could mark a revolutionary change in the biofuel sector and also in the pharmaceutical, nutraceutical and bioremediation industries [191]. Nanomaterials can act as photocatalysts and augment the photosynthetic efficiency of microalgae leading to an increase in biomass. Certain nanomaterials can act as stress inducers, which leads to higher accumulation of lipids. This can be exploited for various benefits. Post cultivation and post lipid extraction from the microalgae, the remaining defatted cells can be used for several purposes supporting “zero-waste technology”. Microalgal residues successfully converted in to green nanomaterials could be used to supplement the microalgal culture system and can also be used for wastewater treatment, biofertilizers and therapeutics [192]. Bhattacharya et al. (2020) investigated the effect of commercial ZnO NPs with algal extract derived ZnO NPs over skin melanoma cells, where algal green synthesized ZnO NPs showed higher reduction in the viability of the skin melanoma cells [193]. Various microalgae derived proteins, polysaccharides, pigments, enzymes etc. are worthwhile for humans and can be included in the diet for health benefits [192]. Hence, a microalga cell can be used to the fullest extent, making the entire process completely technically feasible and economically viable.

Although microalgae promise much, conventional methods used for their cultivation, harvesting and transesterification are not currently commercially viable. Concerns including wastage of water for cultivation, low biomass productivity, slow electron transport must be addressed. Further, the foremost impediments in the large-scale production of microalgae biodiesel are the expensive cultivation system and energy extensive extraction processes [194]. Nanomaterials may present risk in terms of toxicity, whereas effectual recovery and reusability remains a major topic of investigation for securing the sustainability of the proposed technology and potential contamination to algal biomass [195]. As majority of the studies are limited to explore the short term effects of the nanomaterials. Therefore, the fate of nanomaterials in the environment and it’s negative impacts to human health should also be addressed.

Hence, the future investigations should have a focus on evaluating the long term effects, if carried out can help in profiling the exact consequences. Therefore, by proposing suitable guidelines and regulation facilitate in minimizing the associated complications [196]. Though, nanotechnology is a promising approach to address the various bottlenecks such as biomass productivity, harvesting of cells, conversions, transesterification etc in order to fulfil the commercial demands of a microalgae bio-refinery. Optimisation in the properties of nanomaterials is required in terms of size, shape and surface area, which may result in higher yields and lower toxicity levels. A wide range of products can be obtained from microalgae refineries hence, improving the quality and quantity of microalgae-derived is required.

The review attempts to provide insights into the merits and demerits of nanotechnology on various aspects of microalgae such as growth and lipid production for biodiesel applications. Considering the lack of experimental standards and various findings of researchers even among similar algal species, it is challenging to determine the precise effects of nanoparticles on microalgal systems, as these depend on variables such as concentration, particle size, surface charge and exposure time. There is a need for deeper studies of the effects of nanoparticles on microalgae, particularly as toxic effects are reported in some studies. The integration of Nanotechnology and Algal biotechnology has created a whole new science: “Algal-nanobiotechnology”. This review has described the use of complete microalga cell for the development of sustainable technology. The application of various nanomaterials on algal photosynthesis and lipid biosynthesis have been noted, whilst the economic limitations are largely yet to be addressed. However, algal-nanobiotechnology has the potential to obtain high lipid synthesis with convenience and minimal cost. There is a great need to harness the rich algal diversity for various applications including using algal residues to promote zero-waste sustainable technologies.
